coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of water-soluble nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots-polypyrrole nanocomposite via in-situ polymerization for high performance supercapacitor application
coredata.dc:creator: Sharanappa, Sapna
Vijaykumar, S.P.
Suresh, D.S.
Shbil, Abdullah Ba
Ganesha, H.
Veeresh, S.
Nagaraju, Y.S.
Devendrappa, H.
coredata.dc:description: In this work, water-soluble nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) were synthesized via a hydrothermal approach, and nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots-polypyrrole nanocomposites (NCQDs/PPy) were synthesized using In-Situ chemical polymerization methods. The synthesized pure NCQDs, PPy, and NCQDs/PPy nanocomposites are characterized with help of X-ray diffractometry (XRD), Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR), Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FESEM), Ultraviolet-Visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy, and Raman spectroscopy techniques to study their chemical changes, structural phase, morphology, and optical properties. The electrochemical performance is investigated through a two-electrode system, and the NCQDs/PPy-2 nanocomposite shows a high specific capacitance (Csp) of 750 Fg−1 at a current density (CD) of 1 Ag−1 with a wide operating potential window from −0.8 V to 0.8 V. In addition, the fabricated symmetrical device shows an energy density (ED) of 43.47 Whkg−1 and a high-power density (PD) of 3129.84 Wkg−1. Besides, the NCQDs/PPy-2 shows a higher cycle life span with 77.82 % of capacitance retention and 97.04 % of coulombic efficiency even after 8000 charging and discharging cycles. These findings demonstrate that NCQDs/PPy-2 nanocomposite material is a highly prominent electrode material for supercapacitor applications.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of water-soluble nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots-polypyrrole nanocomposite via in-situ polymerization for high performance supercapacitor application

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Mangalore University, Mangalagangothri 574199, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Mangalore University, Mangalagangothri-574199, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, water-soluble nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) were synthesized via a hydrothermal approach, and nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots-polypyrrole nanocomposites (NCQDs/PPy) were synthesized using In-Situ chemical polymerization methods. The synthesized pure NCQDs, PPy, and NCQDs/PPy nanocomposites are characterized with help of X-ray diffractometry (XRD), Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR), Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FESEM), Ultraviolet-Visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy, and Raman spectroscopy techniques to study their chemical changes, structural phase, morphology, and optical properties. The electrochemical performance is investigated through a two-electrode system, and the NCQDs/PPy-2 nanocomposite shows a high specific capacitance (Csp) of 750 Fg−1 at a current density (CD) of 1 Ag−1 with a wide operating potential window from −0.8 V to 0.8 V. In addition, the fabricated symmetrical device shows an energy density (ED) of 43.47 Whkg−1 and a high-power density (PD) of 3129.84 Wkg−1. Besides, the NCQDs/PPy-2 shows a higher cycle life span with 77.82 % of capacitance retention and 97.04 % of coulombic efficiency even after 8000 charging and discharging cycles. These findings demonstrate that NCQDs/PPy-2 nanocomposite material is a highly prominent electrode material for supercapacitor applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the increasing demands of the user population, the enormous proliferation of non-renewable energy sources and the uncertain environmental problems are growing more acute. This has led to an urgent need for alternative and sustainable energy solutions. It helps address both increasing energy demands and environmental concerns. Energy storage devices play a critical role in integrating renewable energy sources [1]. As an exciting new energy storage technology, supercapacitors, which are also considered a form of electrochemical capacitor [2], are a promising alternative to ion storage for next-generation energy storage devices due to their excellent specific power, rapid charge and discharge, long-term sustainability, low cost, and light weight [3]. Based on the charging-discharging mechanism, supercapacitors are generally classified as electric double layer capacitors (EDLC), pseudo capacitors, and hybrid supercapacitors. The EDLC stores energy based on the accumulation of positive and negative ions at the electrode and electrolyte interface, and carbon-based materials such as carbon nanotubes, activated carbon, and graphene are often cited as potential candidates for the EDLC electrode. Simultaneously, the pseudocapacitor stores electrical charge faradaically through an electosorption process using conductive polymers (polypyrrole, polythiophene, polyaniline, 3, 4-propylenedioxythiophene, etc.) and transition metal oxides (NiO, V2O5, RuO2, CeO2, Co2O3, and MnO2, etc.) as electrode materials [4,5]. Hybrid supercapacitors combine the advantages of the above two types of capacitors and exhibit higher electrochemical performance. Recently, pseudocapacitors with rapid and reversible redox reactions are attracting attention due to their higher specific capacitance compared to normal EDLCs, resulting in a growing interest in employing them as supercapacitors. Electrode materials, as the most critical component of supercapacitors, determine supercapacitor performance [6]. A symmetric or asymmetric supercapacitor can be created by employing two distinct electrodes or two identical electrodes. Various electrode materials have been used to develop pseudocapacitors on carbon nanomaterials and conducting polymers. Carbon materials (carbon aerogel, carbon quantum dots, activated carbon, carbon nanotubes, graphene, onion-like carbons, and carbide-derived carbon) are extensively used as electrode materials for supercapacitors due to their large specific surface area, plentiful pore structure, chemical stability, and superior electrical conductivity, and there has been significant research conducted. And is presently being engaged to enhance the electrochemical properties of carbon materials in order to address the unanswered default in the supercapacitor mechanism [7]. When it comes to materials, the most important consideration is cost. Cost in industrial manufacturing always comes before quality. Because of this, several carbon compounds with superior electrochemical performance than activated carbon have been developed; however, the market for carbon electrodes for supercapacitors is still dominated by active carbon. Continuous optimisation of precursors and preparation procedures to lower costs will always be a driving factor in carbon material research and development [8]. But recently, zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials called carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as a prominent material in supercapacitor applications due to their porosity and large surface area [9]. They are used in many technical applications like photocatalysis, chemical sensing, drug delivery, bioimaging, and energy storage due to their small size (<10 nm), superb water solubility, low cost, good photostability, minimum toxicity, excellent conductivity, photon up conversion mechanism, quantum confinement edge effects, and charge transfer by electron excitation performance, which makes them outstanding substitutes in various applications [10,11]. Furthermore, rich surface functionalization, high surface area, porosity, and energy gap transition with rich conjugated π-domains allow carbon quantum dots to be highly productive free radicals, which may help to interconnect with organic molecules such as hydrocarbons, metal ions, aliphatic compounds, aromatic, alicyclic compounds, polymers, biomolecules, and bucketballs for both ion transfer and volume change and serve as a superior conductive agent to boost conductivity and electrochemical performance. This makes CQDs an excellent candidate for the development of highly efficient energy storage devices such as batteries and supercapacitors [12]. However, polypyrrole (PPy) is the most optimistic conjugate polymer compared to polythiophene and polyaniline; therefore, polypyrrole is extensively investigated and is a significant electrode material for pseudocapacitors owing to its numerous advantages, including high specific capacitance, great storage stability, a low-cost processing method, excellent thermal, chemical, and environmental stability, good redox properties, and ease of preparation [13,14]. Furthermore, due to its π -conjugated system, PPy provides large electrical conductivity, resulting in easy charge transfer, and the morphology of PPy provides mesopore-like structures, which create channels that may be favourable for charge internal continuous transportation [15]. Besides this, direct use of bulk PPy as the electrode material frequently faces two major disadvantages: (1) Uncontrollable and inadequate contact of PPy to the electrolyte gives rise to low charge storage ability, thus resulting in poor specific capacity; (2) The repeated galvanostatic charging-discharging operation associated with the continuous inclusion and deinsertion of counteracting ions induces volumetric or arial changes within the chemical network, which leads to the occurrence of volumetric swallowing, shrinking, and splitting as a result of a slow degradation of the device [16,17]. To overcome this problem, we have incorporated NCQDs into polypyrrole and to increase the electrochemical performance of the pseudocapacitive materials, structural modification of the electroactive material, or participation of NCQDs in the charge transfer processes within the electrode material. Modification of PPy with NCQDs leads to their better separation, which in turn allowed them to maintain their high electrical conductivity. Decorating polypyrrole structures with NCQDs significantly increased their dispersibility and porosity, which improved the electrochemical properties of the NCQDs/PPy composite. And the fabricated NCQDs/PPy-2 electrode showed a high specific capacitance of 750 Fg−1 at a CD of 1 Ag−1. Even after 8000 cycles of cyclic charge and discharge, the optimized NCQDs/PPy-2 electrode showed outstanding long-term cyclic stability, with capacitance retention of 77.82 % and Coulombic efficiency of 97.04 %, which is much greater than the previously reported values. From the literature review. CNT. Xuan Jian et al. reported that the CQDs/PPy-NW nanocomposite electrode material showed a specific capacitance of 306 Fg−1 at a current density of 0.5 Ag−1 [18]. Karim Kakaei et al. reported the fabrication of PANI/C-dots nanoparticles, and the composite material shows a specific capacity of 264.6 Fg−1 at a CD of 2.5 Ag−1 for supercapacitors [19]. In recent days, it has been investigated that (Elumalai Dhandapani et al., 2022) carbon quantum dots-reinforced polypyrrole (PPy/CQDs) composite electrodes were prepared via the hybrid electrospray method and had a specific capacity of 174.21 Cg−1 at 1 Ag−1 in 3 M KOH aqueous electrolyte [20]. In this study, we reported the synthesis of water soluble NCQDs using the hydrothermal method and NCQDs/PPy binary nanocomposites through in-situ polymerization techniques. The pure NQCDs, PPy, NCQDs/PPy-1, and NCQDs/PPy-2 nanocomposites are characterized using different characterization techniques. The morphology of PPy (cauliflower arrangement in PPy, sponge-like PPy spheres in NCQDs/PPy nanocomposite) was controlled by the addition of smart NCQDs at different concentrations during the synthesis process. The electrochemical properties of NCQDs/PPy nanocomposite are investigated using a two electrode SSC system with PVA/KOH as a gel electrolyte, and the results are thoroughly discussed. In which NCQDs/PPy-2 nanocomposite shows high Csp, ED, and outstanding long-term cycling stability compared to previously reported studies. The constructed SSCs are used to light a commercial red LED, which connects serially and effectively flashes for 1 to 2 min with frequent voltage drops. These findings demonstrate that NCQDs/PPy-2 nanocomposite material is highly recommended as an electrode material for supercapacitor applications. Furthermore, the findings of this study will aid in the design and development of new electrode materials for supercapacitor applications based on the NCQDs/PPy nanostructured material.
Citric acid (CA) (Sigma-Aldrich, 99 %), urea (CH4N2O), pyrrole monomer, and ferric chloride from Merck India, Deionized (DI) water was used throughout the experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: NCQDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal method. In this process, 4 g of citric acid acts as a precursor, and 4 g of urea is added to 15 ml of DI water. Then the solution was stirred for over 1 h, then transferred into a 50 ml Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and heated in a microwave oven at temperature 180 °C for 24 h. After cooling at room temperature, the resultant brown aqueous mixed solution was filtered, and the final product was dried at 60 °C for 12 h to obtain the NCQD.
Polypyrrole is prepared using an in-situ chemical polymerization technique. In this process,

2 ml of pyrrole monomer was dissolved in 100 ml of 1 M HCl and continuously stirred for 1 h. Then, 11.35 g of oxidizing agent FeCl3 in 100 ml of 1 M HCl was poured into the above mixture (ice bath) with constant stirring for 1 h. The solution was kept for 24 h for polymerization, and the resultant suspension was washed several times with deionized water and dried at 60 °C for 12 h to obtain pure PPy.

The NCQDs/PPy nanocomposite was synthesized by an in-situ chemical oxidation polymerization process. 0.3 M of pyrrole monomer was added to 100 ml of 1 M HCl and stirred for 1 h, followed by the drop-wise addition of 50 ml of NCQDs (1 mg/ml for NCQDs/PPy-1 composite and 2 mg/ml for NCQDs/PPy-2). Then, 0.7 M of the oxidizing agent FeCl3 in 100 ml of 1 M HCl was streamed dropwise into the above solution under an ice bath environment with uniform stirring for one hour. Then the mixed solution was kept for 24 h for polymerization, and the resultant product was washed with deionized water and dried at 60 °C for 12 h to get NCQDs/PPy nanocomposite.

The fabrication of electrode by combining the nanocomposite, PVDF, and activated charcoal with mass proportion of 90:05:05 were added into NMP solvent and thoroughly mixing resultant form is slurry, which was coated on stainless steel and dried at 70 °C for 5 h to evaporate the NMP solvent [21]. In this procedure, 20 ml of deionized water was added to dissolve 2 g of PVA, while another 20 ml of deionized water was added to dissolve 2 g of KOH. Then these two solutions were blended, heated at 80 °C about an hour, and vigorously stirred to produce gel. This gel is used as an electrolyte. By slicing a thin layer of PVA/KOH gel electrolyte between the positive and negative electrodes separated by a Whatman 93 filter paper, the SSC was built and used for further experimental measurement.

XRD patterns of the prepared samples were obtained by using a Rigaku X-ray diffractometer using Cu Kα (λ = 1.5406) as the X-ray radiation at a scan rate of 3/min, from 2θ = 3 to 70°, and generator voltage and current of 40 kV and 30 mA, respectively. FT-IR characterization is done by using an ATR Alpha Bruker instrument with a spectral range of 4000–500 cm−1. The UV–visible spectra were measured using a double-beam Perkin-Elmer Lambda-35 UV–visible spectrophotometer in the spectral range of 190–1100 nm. The Raman spectra were recorded using a He-Ne laser (633 nm) in a HORIBA-JOBIN-YVON Lab RAM HR 800 apparatus in the range 100-3000 cm−1. The surface morphology study was performed using the FESEM Sigma Zeiss, ULTRA 55 instrument and the HRTEM Titan Themis 300 kV from FEI. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area analysis was carried out by recording nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms at 77.35 K (BELSORP II, Japan). Samples were degassed at 200 °C in a vacuum for 5 h, prior to the BET analysis. All the electrochemical characterizations were carried out using the PC based CHI 660E electrochemical workstation.

Fig. 1(a) shows the XRD pattern of NCQDs exhibiting a major diffraction peak at 2θ = 26.5° with a lattice d-spacing of 0.33 nm related to the crystal plane (002). It is clearly noticeable the graphite nature of NCQDs. The diffraction peak found at 2θ = 9° with lattice d-spacing of 0.98 nm is attributed to the (100) crystal plane and verifies the presence of oxygen-rich functional groups on the surface of NCQDs. [22,23].

Fig. 1(a) shows sharp peaks at 2θ =14° and 16°; it is also observed that some other peaks at higher angles with less intensity are indicative of the ordered structure in NCQDs [24]. Fig. 1 (b) reveals a broad characteristic peak located at 2θ =25.8° attributed to the amorphous character of PPy [25]. In Fig. 1(c) the NCQDs/PPy-1 nanocomposite exhibits a broad peak at 26° due to the presence of PPy, which indicates the influence of NCQDs in PPy [26]. 1(d) represent XRD spectra of NCQDs/PPy-2 and show a significant characteristic peak presented 2θ = 26.4° which correspond to the (002) plane due to the presence of PPy and explain the amorphous state of PPy. Furthermore, the enhanced amorphous nature of the NCQDs/PPy-2 composite is attributed to the slight shift from 26° to 26.4°.

Fig. 2(a) shows the FTIR spectra of NCQDs; the characteristic peaks at 1689 cm−1, 1353 cm−1, and 1037 cm−1 are assigned to the oxygenated CO, COC, and COOH functional groups, respectively. The hydrophilic nature of these groups enhanced the solubility and chemical flexibility of the fundamental groups in the NCQDs. Peaks at 1523 cm−1 and 1180 cm−1 are attributed to the non‑oxygenated CC and CNHC functional groups [27].

Fig. 2(b) shows the FT-IR spectra of PPy, and its characteristic peak located at 1530 cm−1 is assigned to the CC ring vibration. The peaks at 1399, 1244, and 1045 cm−1 are due to CN stretching vibration, CN in plane deformation, and CH plane bending vibration [28]. The peak at 945 cm−1 related to CC in-plane bending vibration in the pyrrole ring and 776 cm−1 corresponding to the NH group indicate polymerization of pyrrole. In Fig. 2(c, d), the characteristic peaks observed at 1529, 1306, 1180, 1033, and 910 cm−1 are attributed to the CO, CC, CN in-plane deformations, CH, and CC in-plane bending vibrations in the pyrrole ring respectively, attributed to the NCQDs/PPy-1 and NCQDs/PPy-2 composites. The main peak at 777 cm−1 is due to the polymerization of pyrrole in the NCQDs/PPy composite material [29]. When NCQDs are added, the absorption peaks are not noticeably different from those of pure PPy. This suggests that the NCQDs are well-dispersed within the PPy matrix and do not have a substantial impact on the electrical characteristics of the composite material. In addition, the characteristic peak at 1701 cm−1 attributed to the carbonyl group (CO), which is also observed in the CQD spectra, demonstrates that NCQDs are effectively absorbed on the surface of polypyrrole. The CO stretching vibration in the NCQDs/PPy (1,2) composite shifted from 1698 to 1707 cm−1 due to hydrogen bonding interaction between the carbonyl group of NCQDs and the NH of PPy [30] is clearly represented in Scheme 1 . All the bonds in the FT-IR spectra of the NCQDs/PPy-2 composite superficially shift to higher values of wavenumber as compared to NCQDs/PPy-1 due to proper chemical establishment in the NCQDs/PPy nanocomposites [31]. In Fig. 2 (a, b, c, and d), the peak at about 2350 cm−1 might have come from ambient CO2 due to measurement settings. The peaks at 3621, 3745, and 3856 cm−1 may be attributed to the vibrational stretching of hydrogen-bonded OH functional groups [32].

The UV–visible absorption spectra of NCQDs show two characteristic absorbance peaks located at 235 and 328 nm, as shown in Fig. 3(a). These peaks are typically attributed to the π-π* transition due to CC bonds and the n-π* transition due to CO bonds [33]. In addition, the visible band explains the presence of amino groups on the surface of NCQDs.

The absorption tail at 500 nm explains, in part, that carbon quantum dots are nitrogen doped [34]. The NCQDs in aqueous solution show blue luminescence on exposure to UV light at 365 nm, as shown in the inset of Fig. 3(a). This UV–vis data demonstrated that NCQDs were formed. [9]. Fig. 3(b) shows the UV–visible spectra of pure PPy and displays two distinct peaks at 386 nm related to π-π* transition and 587 nm due to bipolaronic absorption, which reveals the oxidized states of PPy. The absorption bands at 273 and 502 nm are due to the presence of the amino group of PPY in the NCQDs/PPy-1 composite, as shown in Fig. 3(c) [35]. In addition, between 400 and 600 nm, there was a substantial absorption tail known as the free carrier tail, which arises when electrons are conjugated along the polymeric chain and represents that the chains are extended, not randomly coiled, but dispersed homogeneously [36]. In Fig. 3(d), it is seen that the absorption peaks of the NCQDs/PPy-2 composite are shifted to 282 from 273 nm and 536 from 502 nm, as compared to the NCQDs/PPy-1 composite. This shift in the position of absorption peaks suggests that the incorporation of NCQDs in the PPy matrix has caused a slight redshift in the absorption spectrum of the NCQDs/PPy-2 composite. This could be attributed to changes in the electronic structure or intermolecular interactions within the composite material [37].

The Raman spectra give a clear idea about the chemical interaction and change, as shown in Fig. 4 . It is seen that two main bands at 1360 and 1596 cm−1 are associated with the disordered character of the carbon D band in NCQDs (SP3 defect), and the in-plane vibration of SP2 carbon causes the graphite carbon's G band, as cited in Fig. 4(a) [38]. The Ramana spectra of PPy show two primary scattering bands at 1353 and 1569 cm−1 due to the polymer backbone's ring vibration stretching phase and conjugated π structure, which are designated to the D and G bands, respectively, as shown in Fig. 4(b). The peak appeared at 1050 cm−1 is assigned to the CH in plane deformation, and peaks at 963 and 637 cm−1 with low intensities indirectly explain the quinoid bipolaronic structure of PPy's ring deformation.

In Fig. 4(c) the broad peaks in the NCQDs/PPy-1 composite that appeared at 1359 and 1564 cm−1 are ascribed to G and D band present in the NCQDs. The interaction between PPy and NCQDs in the NCQDs/PPy-2 composite has also resulted in an enhancement of the intensities of the D and G bands at 1355 and 1580 cm−1 as cited in Fig. 4(d). This suggests that the composite material has successfully regained the intrinsic properties of the NCQDs, as observed in previous studies. The enhanced intensities further support the presence of strong intermolecular interactions within the composite. The intensity ratio of the D and G bands indicates the degree of defects present in the material. The values of the intensity ratio ID/IG for NCQDS/PPy-2 nanocomposite are 0.92 and 1.12 for PPy; this explains why the incorporation of NCQDs on PPy has decreased the number of defects in PPy [41]. These outcomes concur with the FT-IR outcomes.

The SEM images of pure PPy, NCQDs/PPy-2 and the NCQDs/PPy-2 composite are displayed in Fig. 5 . Pure PPy image a possessed a cauliflower arrangement extended uniform size is formed and are not well segregated as shown in Fig. 5(a and b) [43]. Fig. 5 (c and d) demonstrate SEM images of NCQDs/PPy-1 nanocomposite, in which it is observed that the large cauliflower arrangement is that of pure PPy; this attests to pyrrole's polymerization, and on the surface of PPy, there is also a little spherical structure, which confirms NCQDs are readily absorbed on the PPy surface. [44]. Fig. 5(e and f) shows a FESEM image of NCQDs/PPy-2 nanocomposite, in which the PPy spheres are well separated and provide large porosity, which could help to enhance the surface area as compared to NCQDs/PPy-1 (due to the higher concentration of NCQDs in the NCQDs/PPy-2 nanocomposite), resulting in the surface of PPy becoming harder after incorporation of NCQDs [45].

FESEM results indicate that the addition of NCQDs has a substantially impact on the PPy's pore structure in NCQDs/PPy nanocomposites, i.e., it is observed sponge-like PPy spheres in the NCQDs/PPy nanocomposite and it is enabling the development of porous structures, whereas a sphere-like structure is observed in PPy. Due to the existence of a huge number of hydrophilic chemical groups in the NCQDs, they provide a surfactant to enhance the conductivity and optimise the porous structures of PPy in NCQDs/PPy nanocomposites [46].

The morphology and inner structure of as-prepared NCQDs and NCQDs/PPy nanocomposite are exhibited by HR-TEM measurement. Fig. 6(a) reveals that the NCQDs are nearly spherical and possess uniform dispersion without aggregation [47]. The inset in Fig. 6(a) shows the well-resolved distinct parallel lattice planes in the NCQDs, indicating their crystalline nature with an interfringe spacing of 0.2 nm, which correlates to the (102) crystal plane of graphitic carbon [48]. This graphitic-like structure might be attributed to the existence of a sp2 carbon (002) plane, which was consistent with the XRD peak detected at 2 = 26°. The selective area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern shows a prominent concentric ring pattern indicating the planes with graphitic-like structure as shown in Fig. 6(b) [49]. Fig. 6(c) shows the size distribution histogram obtained by the Gaussian fitting curve based on counting >100 particles, and most of the NCQDs are distributed in the range of 3 to 7.5 nm with an average diameter of 4.5 nm, which corresponds to their quantum size [50].

The TEM images of NCQDs/PPy-2 nanocomposite shown in Fig. 6(d) reveal the size of particles ∼75 nm with apparent aggregation between particles due to the effect of the electron beam on the highly feeble polymer shells, and this form of polymer-coated nanoparticle aggregation has already been observed in the literature. [51]. PPy shows chain-like polymer morphology with no regular bulges on the surface and is composed of a huge number of amorphous particles, and NCQDs were embedded into the PPy matrix, as clearly shown in Fig. 6(e). It's possible that these surface alterations result from the functional groups of NCQDs interacting with the PPy matrix. [52]. Fig. 6(f) shows that the broad circular rings indicate the amorphous nature of the NCQDs/PPy-2 nanocomposite [26].

Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms were used to obtain information about the BET surface area and pore size distribution in the synthesized NCQDs/PPy composites. The N2 adsorption/desorption isotherm of NCQDs/PPy composites exhibited hysteresis loop at relative pressure (P/P0) close to unity is shown in Fig. 7(a), indicating the presence of large mesopores and macropores, which can be assorted as type IV isotherm exhibited H3 hysteresis loop associated with slit like pores [53].

The variation of isotherm and pore size distribution plots demonstrates by varying the NCQDs concentration, the specific surface area of NCQDs/PPy-1 and NCQDs/PPy-2 nanocomposite was 13.9 m2g−1 and 18.2 m2g−1 respectively. The Barrett- Joyner-Halenda (BJH) study as shown in Fig. 7(b), reveals average pore sizes of NCQDs/PPy-1 and NCQDs/PPy-2 is 1.29 and 2.12 nm respectively. The larger surface area and higher pore volume of NCQDs/PPy nanocomposite offer more electroactive sites for energy storage and are favourable as a potential candidate for high-performance supercapacitor application [54]. And these results also agree with the FESEM analysis.

Fig. 8 . shows the CV curves of NCQDs, PPy, NCQDs/PPy-1, and NCQDs/PPy-2 nanocomposites electrodes operated from a − 0.8 V to 0.8 V potential window. The CV curves of NCQDs in Fig. 8(a), PPy in Fig. 8(b) and NCQDs/PPy nanocomposite in Fig. 8(c and d) displays a good quasi-rectangular shape irrespective of whether the sweep rates are high or low; this implies that as the scan rate increases, the electrodes regain their identical shape without any distortion, which clearly demonstrates the excellent electrochemical stability and ideal reversibility performance of the electrode [55]. Both polymers and NCQDs/PPy composites exhibit redox-couple type CV curves in this potential range, indicating pseudocapacitance behaviour. The current under CV curves increases with increasing the scan rate from 10 to 50 mVs−1. As the scan rate increases, the potential of the anodic and cathodic peaks shifts slightly in the more positive and negative directions, respectively. This shows that the voltametric currents are directly proportional to the scan rate and thereby display the capacitive behaviour of nanocomposite [56,57]. From the electrochemical analysis, one can conclude that the NCQDs/PPy-2 is the best electrode material for energy harvesting with more electroactive site zones as compared to the NCQDs/PPy-1, PPy, and NCQDs electrodes as revealed in Fig. 8(d).

The specific capacitance was calculated from the CV curves by using the below equation [58].
(1)
Csp =
∫ IdV
m × ∆ V × ϑ
where, Csp (Fg−1) is specific capacitance, I is current response, ∆ V is the voltage window, ϑ
is scan rate and m is mass of the deposited electrode material.

The specific capacitances for NCQDs, PPy, NCQDs/PPy-1, and NCQDs/PPy-2 are 89, 196, 306, and 555 Fg−1 at a scan rate of 10 mVs−1 respectively. The specific capacitance for NCQDs/PPy-2 is high as compared to NCQDs/PPy-1, PPy, and NCQDs due to the incorporation of NCQDs into PPy, which enhances the electroactive sites and accelerates the charge transfer rate. In addition, the greater electrical conductivity of the PPy provides a fast diffusion path for electron transportation through the electrolyte medium as well as their interface. Further, it can be observed that the Csp is directly proportional to the area under CV curves, and Fig. 8(e) provides evidence for this at a scan rate of 10 mVs−1, which suggests a high storage capacity of NCQDs/PPy-2 nanocomposite [59]. Furthermore, Fig. 8(f) shows CV curves for the NCQDs/PPy-2 nanocomposite electrode at different Voltage window at a scan rate of 10 mVs−1 to determine the operating potential voltage limit and display symmetrical cyclic voltammograms without hydrogen or oxygen evolution over the entire potential window, which explains the stability of the electrode as an increase in the voltage window [60]. The comparison bar graphs show that Csp decreases as the scan rate increases, as shown in Fig. 8 (g). This phenomenon may be due to the internal resistance of the electrode or the mass transport limitation of ions from the electrolyte to the electrode surface to satisfy electronic neutralization at the electrode during the faradaic reaction [61,62].

Fig. 8. reveal GCD curves of NCQDs, PPy, NCQDs/PPy-1, and NCQDs/PPy-2 nanocomposite at different current densities from 1 to 3 Ag−1. All the GCD curves display a nearly symmetric triangular shape, which confirms the pseudocapacitive behaviour of electrodes [63]. High current densities cause a higher concentration of charge carriers, leading to an increase in the resistance of the electrolyte and a decrease in the effective diffusion coefficient [64]. The GCD curves of NCQDs in Fig. 9(a) show a triangular shape with little internal resistance drop, suggesting the NCQDs have somewhat high internal resistance as compared to other prepared electrodes. Fig. 9(c) reveals GCD curves showing NCQDs/PPy-1 nanocomposite has a higher discharge time as compared to pure PPy, as illustrated in Fig. 9(b) with almost no obvious IR drop, indicating electrodes have a small internal resistance and therefore store energy efficiently due to the incorporation of carbon quantum dots, which provide more electroactive sites on the surface of PPy and enhance the stability of NCQDs/PPy-1 nanocomposite electrode. The GCD curves of NCQDs/PPy-2 composite show approximately symmetric curves with a small internal resistance drop, which confirms the material's excellent coulombic efficiency and electrochemical reversibility as shown in Fig. 9(d) [65]. In addition, the IR drops were due to the presence of internal resistance in the electrode associated with the electrical connection resistance, bulk solution resistance and resistance of ion migration in the electrode, which contributed to the non-linear discharge curves [21].

The specific capacitance of each electrode is estimated from discharging curve by using below equation [66].
(2)
Csp =
I × ∆ t
m × ∆ V
where, Csp (Fg−1) is specific capacitance, I is current, ∆t is discharge time, m is active mass of the electrode and ∆V is the discharge potential window.

The calculated specific capacitances of NCQDs, PPy, NCQDs/PPy-1, and NCQDs/PPy-2 are 135, 185, 380, and 750 Fg−1 at a CD of 1 Ag−1 respectively. It is noticed that NCQDs/PPy-2 nanocomposite display the highest specific capacitance due to the large surface area and the rapid diffusion and migration of ions between electrolyte and electrode interfaces [67]. In addition, the discharging time of the NCQDs/PPy-2 nanocomposite is longer as compared to NCQDs, PPy, and NCQDs/PPy-1, indicating greater charge storage performance due to the presence of NCQDs (the amount of NCQD concentration is higher as compared to the NCQDs/PPy-1 nanocomposite) in PPy [68]. Because, in the synthesis process, citric acid plays the main role as a carbon source and provides support to the reactive site to react with urea, the concentration of NCQDs increases in the NCQDs/PPy nanocomposite, which enhances the surface properties of PPy and results in a high specific capacitance. It could be well agreed that the morphology of the NCQDs/PPy-2 nanocomposite is well separated and provides a larger surface area, as is clearly evidenced from the BET analysis. The corresponding graph of specific capacitance with current density is shown in Fig. 9(e). The capacitance value decreases due to the increase of current density, as does the internal resistance of the electrode. Ions can easily penetrate and make contact with the entire electrode material at lower current density, but with increasing current density, the permeation of the ions deceases and the material at the outer surface of the electrode mainly contributes to the overall capacitance [69]. And these results also agree with the CV results.

Further to explore the ionic and electronic transport pathways of NCQDs, PPy, NCQDs/PPy-1, and NCQDs/PPy-2 SSC devices, the EIS studies were carried out. Representing Z′ along the X and -Z′′ along the Y-axis were plotted in the frequency region of 0.1 Hz to 100 kHz. Fig. 10 . shows Nyquist plots of NCQDs, PPy, NCQDs/PPy-1, and NCQDs/PPy-2 nanocomposite which were used to examine the charging and discharging process of electrode materials. The high frequency region of the Nyquist plot in which the diameter of the quasi-semicircle represents the charge transfer resistance (Rct), which is related to the interfacial process at the counter electrode electrolyte interface [70].

The Rct values of NCQDs, PPy, NCQDs/PPy-1, and NCQDs/PPy-2 nanocomposites electrodes are 14.74, 13.11, 12.23, and 11.81 Ω respectively. Additionally, a straight line is seen at the high frequency zone after the semicircle region, coupled in parallel with the double-layer capacitance and the charge transfer resistance. (Rct correspond to the semicircle diameter). The solution resistance (Rs) is given by the line intercept with Z′ along the X-axis at high frequency region and it explains the electrolyte and electrode resistance, and the contact resistance at the interface between current collector and active material [71]. The Rs values of NCQDs, PPy, NCQDs/PPy-1, and NCQDs/PPy-2 nanocomposites electrodes are 0.54, 0.50, 0.44, and 0.40 Ω respectively [72]. The smaller Rs values of prepared electrodes indirectly explains strong charge diffusion process established between electrode and substrate material, as a resulting lower inner resistance of electrodes. After high frequency region of Nyquist plot, it is observing a vertical line in the low frequency region which is corresponds to the Warburg impedance (impedance of redox electrolytes) resulting from the frequency dependence of ion diffusion/transport between electrode-electrolyte interfaces [73]. In addition, the Rct value of NCQDs/PPy-2 was estimated to be 11.81 Ω which is comparably smaller than that of NCQDs/PPy-1(12.23 Ω) and PPy (13.11 Ω) nanocomposite, which confirms the excellent rate capabilities and quick charge transfer kinetics. The inset of Fig. 10. shows the equivalent circuit for NCQDs/PPy-2 electrode according to the Randle equivalent circuit fitting method. The Cdl and the Rct are connected in series with Rs. The diffusion element W is in series with the Rct, which corresponds to the diffusion from the high to the low frequency region. Normally, an ideal electrode with mass capacitance C at very low frequencies, should lead to a vertical line parallel to the –Z″ axis. The given equivalent circuit was responsible for the electrode processes at the interphase between the composite material and the solution. Rct is the charge transfer resistance related to polymer electro-oxidation, W is the Warburg impedance related to counterions transport accompanying the polymer oxidation process and compensating for the positive charge formed on the composite layer, and Cdl is the double-layer capacitance of the external interphase between the composite and electrolyte [75].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performance of NCQDs/PPy-2 symmetric supercapacitor device
ce:section.ce:para: Symmetric supercapacitor device fabricated for practical applications by using composite NCQDs/PPy-2 electrodes, separated by the gel electrolyte (PVA-KOH). The cycle stability of the SSC electrode material is an essential parameter for assessing its potential for practical application. Fig. 11(a) shows the capacity retention and Coulombic efficiency as assessed by GCD charging and discharging for NCQDs/PPy-2 SCC device. As a result, SSC device displays long stability cycling and greater energy storage systems as a supercapacitive electrode material. All curves are linear and symmetrical, and there is no discernible IR drop, demonstrating 8000 cycles stability at a CD of 3 Ag−1.

The Coulombic efficiency is often used to assess the cycle stability of the electrode materials and devices. and it can be calculated using the following equation [62].
(3)
η =
Δtd
∆ tc
× 100 %
where ∆td and ∆tc are the discharge and charge times in second.

The SSC device offers good cyclic reliability with a coulombic efficiency of 97.04 % and 77.82 % capacitance retention observed after 8000 cycles at a current density of 3 Ag−1, indicating that the material is electrochemically stable. The inset of Fig. 10(a) shows the first 10 cycles and the last 10 cycles, demonstrating strong GCD characteristics and long-term cyclic stability, which might extend the life of the storage system [76]. Moreover, for the reusability of any electrical equipment, the chemical stability as well as the nanostructures of the fabricating material should retain their stability and morphology after many cycles of operation [69]. To verify this point, we provide the FTIR study, FESEM, and EIS analysis of the NCQDs/PPy-2 SSC device after 8000 cycles. The surface of the NCQDs/PPy-2 electrode employed in the SSC was analysed by FTIR to understand any chemical change on its surface, as shown in Fig. 11(b). The FTIR study revealed no significant chemical changes on the surface of the NCQDs/PPy-2 electrode after 8000 cycles, indicating its chemical stability [77]. Additionally, The FESEM images reveal that the electrode maintains its structural integrity and retains a well-defined surface morphology, indicating its stability and durability throughout the cycling process as shown in Fig. 11(c) [78]. The Nyquist plot of the SSC device before and after 8000 cycles is shown in Fig. 11(d). EIS patterns show nearly negligible variation, thereby confirming the stability of the SSC device. The Rct values varied from 11.81 to 13.98 Ω and the Rs values also increased from 0.40 to 0.65 Ω at the high frequency region after 8000 cycles. The reason may be due to the decrease in electrical conductivity during the cycling process [79,80]. However, electrochemical performance depends on many factors like the concentration of mass of the active material, electrolyte, area and thickness of the electrode, etc., and also the measuring parameters, such as the scan rates and current densities. So, on the basis of an overall study of the two-electrode system, a rough comparison with data from some reports of NCQDs/PPy composite materials is shown in Table 1 with references.

Energy density and power density are the two key metrics to evaluate the performance of a SC device. The ED and PD are calculated from GCD curve using the below equations [82].
(4)
E =
C sp
∆
V 2
7.2
Wh
kg
− 1
(5)
P =
3600 × E
t
W
kg
− 1
where Csp (Fg−1) is computed specific capacitance using GCD curves, ∆V and t are the change in potential and discharge time respectively.

Fig. 12(a) shows Ragone plots of the NCQDs/PPy-2 SSC device and detailed comparison with other SC systems. As, demonstrated, SSC device exhibits the maximum energy density of 43.47 Whkg−1 with power density of 3129.84 Wkg−1, which is much higher than that of the previously reported CQDs based symmetric and asymmetric supercapacitors like, NCQDs/CoS//rGO (36.6 Whkg−1 at PD of 800 Wkg−1) [83], CQDs/Ni2S2/NF (18.8 Whkg−1 at PD of 134 Wkg−1) [84], rGH/CDs (35.3 Whkg−1 at PD of 516 Wkg−1) [85], and CQDs/MnO2(36.6 Whkg−1 at PD of 1000 Wkg−1) [76], which confirms the overall remarkable performance of NCQDs/PPy-2 SSC. To evaluate the practical application of the NCQDs/PPy-2 symmetric supercapacitor device, we assembled the two SSC devices in series to glow the red LED as shown in Fig. 12(c), and also exhibit the voltage of 1.59 V shown in Fig. 12(d), which confirms their energy storage application. These results illustrate the suitability of NCQD/PPy composite electrode materials for application in high-performance supercapacitors.

In summary, we have synthesized NCQDs and NCQDs/PPy nanocomposite using hydrothermal and In-situ chemical polymerization methods. The fabricated SSC device shows a high Csp, great flexibility, outstanding performance and extended lifespan due to superior electrochemical properties, strengthening and synergistic actions between NCQDs and PPy in NCQDs/PPy nanocomposites. The NCQDs/PPy-2 electrode exhibit high Csp of 750 Fg−1 at CD of 1 Ag−1 and ED of 43.47 Whkg−1 at PD of 3129.84 Wkg−1. Further, it demonstrates an outstanding Coulombic efficiency of 97.04 % and capacitance retention of 77.82 % even after 8000 cycles at a CD of 3 Ag−1. As a result of their superior electrochemical performance, high specific energy, high specific power, and affordable qualities, it is confirmed that NCQDs/PPy-2 nanocomposite is a desirable prominent candidate to other energy storage devices like batteries, sensors, and other applications in the future.
